0.text: I, Enos, knew my father was a just man. He taught me in his language and in the training and instruction of the Lord — and blessed be the name of my God for it.
I will tell you about the spiritual struggle I had before God, before I received a forgiveness of my sins.
I went out to hunt animals in the forests. The words my father had often spoken about eternal life and the joy of the saints sank deep into my heart.

My soul hungered. I kneeled down before my Maker and cried to him in mighty prayer and pleading for my own soul. All day long I cried to him — and when night came, I still raised my voice high so it reached the heavens.
A voice came to me, saying, "Enos, your sins are forgiven, and you will be blessed."
I, Enos, knew that God could not lie. Therefore, my guilt was swept away.
I said, "Lord, how is this possible?"
He said to me, "Because of your faith in Christ, whom you have never before heard or seen. Many years will pass before he shows himself in the flesh. So go — your faith has made you whole."
When I heard these words, I began to feel a desire for the well-being of my people, the Nephites. I poured out my whole soul to God for them.

While I was struggling in the spirit, the voice of the Lord came into my mind again, saying, "I will visit your people according to how diligently they keep my commandments. I have given them this land, and it is a holy land. I will not curse it unless it is because of wickedness. I will visit your people as I have said, and I will bring their sins down upon their own heads with sorrow."
After I, Enos, heard these words, my faith in the Lord became unshakable. I prayed to him with many long struggles for my brothers, the Lamanites.
After I had prayed and worked with all diligence, the Lord said to me, "I will grant your request because of your faith."

This was the desire I had — that if my people, the Nephites, should fall into sin and somehow be destroyed, and the Lamanites were not destroyed, then the Lord God would preserve a record of my people, the Nephites. Even if it required the power of his holy arm, perhaps it could be brought to the Lamanites at some future time so they might be brought to salvation.

At that time, our efforts to bring the Lamanites to the true faith were useless. They swore in their anger that if possible, they would destroy our records and us, and all the traditions of our fathers.
Knowing that the Lord God could preserve our records, I cried to him continually. He had told me, "Whatever you ask in faith, believing you will receive in the name of Christ, you will receive it."
I had faith, and I cried to God to preserve the records. He made a covenant with me that he would bring them forth to the Lamanites in his own due time.
I, Enos, knew it would happen according to the covenant he made. My soul was at rest.
The Lord said to me, "Your ancestors also asked me for this. It will be done for them according to their faith, for their faith was like yours."
I, Enos, went among the people of Nephi, prophesying about things to come and testifying of the things I had heard and seen.

I testify that the people of Nephi tried hard to bring the Lamanites back to the true faith in God. But our efforts were useless. Their hatred was fixed, and they were led by their evil nature. They became wild, fierce, and bloodthirsty — full of idol worship and filthiness. They fed on animals they hunted, lived in tents, and wandered in the wilderness with short animal-skin clothing around their waists and their heads shaved. Their skill was with the bow, the curved sword, and the axe. Many of them ate nothing but raw meat, and they were constantly trying to destroy us.
The people of Nephi farmed the land and raised all kinds of grain, fruit, flocks, herds, cattle of every kind, goats, wild goats, and many horses.
There were a great many prophets among us. The people were stubborn and hard to teach.

Nothing except extremely harsh preaching and prophesying about wars, conflicts, destructions, and constantly reminding them of death, eternity, the judgments and power of God — with extremely plain speech — could keep them from rushing to destruction. That is how I write about them.
I saw wars between the Nephites and Lamanites during my lifetime.
I began to grow old. One hundred and seventy-nine years had passed since our father Lehi left Jerusalem.

I saw that I must soon go to my grave. I had been compelled by the power of God to preach and prophesy to this people and declare the word according to the truth that is in Christ. I have declared it all my days and have rejoiced in it more than anything in the world.

I will soon go to my place of rest, which is with my Redeemer. I know that in him I will find rest. I rejoice in the day when my mortal body will put on immortality and I will stand before him. Then I will see his face with joy, and he will say to me, "Come to me, you who are blessed. There is a place prepared for you in the mansions of my Father." Amen.
